sourceBasis: exercise_card_details_for_llm.md Card v2 authoring structure and Prepare & Access / Mobilize sequencing
User-provided mobility focus: dynamic movements that open gait, hips, back, shoulders, and neck for workouts and games
Purpose.txt was requested but not found in available File Library search results during this run
cluster.programmingIntent: Flexibility combined with motion: open usable ranges, improve readiness, and reduce compensation without creating meaningful fatigue before high-output work.

cards.name: Walking Knee Hug to Calf Raise
cards.cardSummary: Dynamic mobility drill that opens hip flexion, glute length, foot tripod, and ankle stiffness. Use it when the athlete needs usable range of motion that carries into warm-ups, workouts, or games.
cards.bestPlacement: Prepare & Access phase after a brief temperature raise and before higher-intent skill, speed, strength, or sport work that needs hip flexion, glute length, foot tripod, and ankle stiffness.
cards.description: Walk forward, pull one knee toward the chest, rise into a controlled calf raise on the stance leg, then step through into the next rep.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Mobilize. Coach slow-to-smooth motion, clean breathing, and position ownership. This is flexibility combined with motion; stop before the athlete turns it into fatigue, passive stretching, or sloppy range chasing.
cards.athleteLanguage: Move smoothly, breathe through the range, and own the position you create.
cards.whyItWorks: The drill exposes hip flexion, glute length, foot tripod, and ankle stiffness while the athlete is actively controlling posture, breathing, and transitions. That turns available range into sport-usable mobility instead of passive flexibility that disappears once movement speed increases.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Prepare & Access / Mobilize because it increases movement access and readiness before intensity, load, sprinting, cutting, jumping, tumbling, or gameplay. It should not create meaningful fatigue before Output or skill work.
cards.commonMisuse: Rushing for reps, forcing end range through pinching, or turning the drill into a conditioning station. The goal is smoother access and control, not exhaustion or passive stretching for time.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by range, support, tempo, and total volume before adding complexity. Athletes should keep breathing, alignment, and repeatable motion as the progression standard.

movementRequirements.breathing_demand: controlled nasal or relaxed diaphragmatic breathing

coachingExecution.movement_description: Walk forward, pull one knee toward the chest, rise into a controlled calf raise on the stance leg, then step through into the next rep.
setup: Start in a pain-free range with enough space to move slowly and reset between reps.
Set the ribs over the pelvis and keep the neck relaxed before initiating movement.
Use support, a wall, a mat, or a shorter range if balance or joint comfort limits quality.
Bias the drill toward hip flexion, glute length, foot tripod, and ankle stiffness, not toward maximum speed or fatigue.
execution_steps: Move into the first position slowly enough to feel where range is available.
Breathe through the transition and keep the target joints moving without borrowing motion from the low back or neck.
Pause briefly at the usable end range, then return or step through with the same alignment.
Repeat only while the motion gets cleaner or stays clean; stop when compensation increases.
coach_cues: Smooth first, bigger second.
Breathe into the range; do not force it.
Own the position before you move out of it.
Keep the motion where the drill is supposed to happen.
athlete_cues: Slow is smooth; smooth becomes athletic.
No pinching, no rushing, no sloppy reps.
Move like this range has to work in a game.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the transition if needed; exhale gently into the new range.
Keep the jaw and neck relaxed.
Do not hold the breath to force range.
common_faults: Chasing a bigger range by twisting through the low back or collapsing posture.
Rushing transitions until mobility turns into uncontrolled momentum.
Ignoring pinching, numbness, dizziness, or joint irritation.
Letting balance demands hide the actual mobility target.
quality_gate: No sharp pain or joint pinch.
Breathing stays calm and controlled.
The target joint action is visible without major compensation.
Final rep looks as controlled as the first rep.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or escalating discomfort.
Loss of posture for more than one rep in a row.
Athlete turns the drill into speed, fatigue, or competition instead of mobility.
Range decreases or compensation increases as reps continue.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use smaller ranges, fewer moving parts, and clear start/finish positions. Prioritize play-like smoothness without forcing depth or speed.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add a slightly longer path or step-through rhythm only when posture and breathing stay organized.
scaling.teen: Connect the drill to the sport or lift that follows. Keep the intent crisp and avoid turning warm-up mobility into fatigue volume.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use support and conservative range until the athlete can identify the intended joint action and avoid compensations.
scaling.adult_advanced: Add end-range pauses, active lift-offs, or flowing transitions while preserving low RPE and clean mechanics.
scaling.older_adult: Use extra support, smaller ranges, slower tempo, and longer setup time. Avoid floor transitions when getting up and down is the limiting factor.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use clinician guidance when relevant. Avoid breath holding, uncomfortable prone/supine positions, and pressure symptoms; choose supported standing or side-lying options when needed.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by joint history, symptoms, sport demands, training age, confidence, and available range rather than gender.
doNotUseWhen: Sharp pain, joint pinching, numbness, tingling, or dizziness appears.
The athlete cannot control the starting position even after regression.
The session needs immediate high-output work and this drill would become a long, fatigue-creating block.
The athlete treats the drill as passive stretching or a race instead of active mobility.
mediaInternalNotes: Film one clean regression and one clean progression.
Emphasize position-access work, not passive stretching for its own sake.
Best before related strength, speed, skill, field, court, tumbling, or game prep.

readiness_checks: No sharp pain, numbness, tingling, or dizziness before starting.
Athlete can breathe calmly and control the starting position.
Athlete has enough space and support to perform the drill without rushing.
contraindications: Acute joint injury in the target region without clearance.
Symptoms that increase with the drill after range is reduced.
Loss of balance or support that makes the position unsafe.
common_substitutions: Supported Knee Hug to Heel Raise

cards.description: Move from a shin box position to a tall-kneeling or half-kneeling rise, then return to the floor with control.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Mobilize. Coach slow-to-smooth motion, clean breathing, and position ownership. This is flexibility combined with motion; stop before the athlete turns it into fatigue, passive stretching, or sloppy range chasing.
cards.athleteLanguage: Move smoothly, breathe through the range, and own the position you create.

movementRequirements.breathing_demand: controlled nasal or relaxed diaphragmatic breathing

coachingExecution.movement_description: Move from a shin box position to a tall-kneeling or half-kneeling rise, then return to the floor with control.
setup: Start in a pain-free range with enough space to move slowly and reset between reps.
Set the ribs over the pelvis and keep the neck relaxed before initiating movement.
Use support, a wall, a mat, or a shorter range if balance or joint comfort limits quality.
Bias the drill toward hip rotation, trunk control, and ground-to-kneel transition, not toward maximum speed or fatigue.
execution_steps: Move into the first position slowly enough to feel where range is available.
Breathe through the transition and keep the target joints moving without borrowing motion from the low back or neck.
Pause briefly at the usable end range, then return or step through with the same alignment.
Repeat only while the motion gets cleaner or stays clean; stop when compensation increases.
coach_cues: Smooth first, bigger second.
Breathe into the range; do not force it.
Own the position before you move out of it.
Keep the motion where the drill is supposed to happen.
athlete_cues: Slow is smooth; smooth becomes athletic.
No pinching, no rushing, no sloppy reps.
Move like this range has to work in a game.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the transition if needed; exhale gently into the new range.
Keep the jaw and neck relaxed.
Do not hold the breath to force range.
common_faults: Chasing a bigger range by twisting through the low back or collapsing posture.
Rushing transitions until mobility turns into uncontrolled momentum.
Ignoring pinching, numbness, dizziness, or joint irritation.
Letting balance demands hide the actual mobility target.
quality_gate: No sharp pain or joint pinch.
Breathing stays calm and controlled.
The target joint action is visible without major compensation.
Final rep looks as controlled as the first rep.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or escalating discomfort.
Loss of posture for more than one rep in a row.
Athlete turns the drill into speed, fatigue, or competition instead of mobility.
Range decreases or compensation increases as reps continue.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use smaller ranges, fewer moving parts, and clear start/finish positions. Prioritize play-like smoothness without forcing depth or speed.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add a slightly longer path or step-through rhythm only when posture and breathing stay organized.
scaling.teen: Connect the drill to the sport or lift that follows. Keep the intent crisp and avoid turning warm-up mobility into fatigue volume.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use support and conservative range until the athlete can identify the intended joint action and avoid compensations.
scaling.adult_advanced: Add end-range pauses, active lift-offs, or flowing transitions while preserving low RPE and clean mechanics.
scaling.older_adult: Use extra support, smaller ranges, slower tempo, and longer setup time. Avoid floor transitions when getting up and down is the limiting factor.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use clinician guidance when relevant. Avoid breath holding, uncomfortable prone/supine positions, and pressure symptoms; choose supported standing or side-lying options when needed.

cards.name: Dowel Shoulder Pass-Through
cards.cardSummary: Dynamic mobility drill that opens shoulder flexion-extension arc, pec length, and rib control. Use it when the athlete needs usable range of motion that carries into warm-ups, workouts, or games.
cards.bestPlacement: Prepare & Access phase after a brief temperature raise and before higher-intent skill, speed, strength, or sport work that needs shoulder flexion-extension arc, pec length, and rib control.
cards.description: Hold a wide dowel grip and slowly move the dowel from the front of the body to overhead and behind the body without rib flare or pinching.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Mobilize. Coach slow-to-smooth motion, clean breathing, and position ownership. This is flexibility combined with motion; stop before the athlete turns it into fatigue, passive stretching, or sloppy range chasing.
cards.athleteLanguage: Move smoothly, breathe through the range, and own the position you create.
cards.whyItWorks: The drill exposes shoulder flexion-extension arc, pec length, and rib control while the athlete is actively controlling posture, breathing, and transitions. cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Prepare & Access / Mobilize because it increases movement access and readiness before intensity, load, sprinting, cutting, jumping, tumbling, or gameplay. It should not create meaningful fatigue before Output or skill work.
cards.commonMisuse: Rushing for reps, forcing end range through pinching, or turning the drill into a conditioning station. The goal is smoother access and control, not exhaustion or passive stretching for time.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by range, support, tempo, and total volume before adding complexity. Athletes should keep breathing, alignment, and repeatable motion as the progression standard.

movementRequirements.breathing_demand: controlled nasal or relaxed diaphragmatic breathing

coachingExecution.movement_description: Hold a wide dowel grip and slowly move the dowel from the front of the body to overhead and behind the body without rib flare or pinching.
setup: Start in a pain-free range with enough space to move slowly and reset between reps.
Set the ribs over the pelvis and keep the neck relaxed before initiating movement.
Use support, a wall, a mat, or a shorter range if balance or joint comfort limits quality.
Bias the drill toward shoulder flexion-extension arc, pec length, and rib control, not toward maximum speed or fatigue.
execution_steps: Move into the first position slowly enough to feel where range is available.
Breathe through the transition and keep the target joints moving without borrowing motion from the low back or neck.
Pause briefly at the usable end range, then return or step through with the same alignment.
Repeat only while the motion gets cleaner or stays clean; stop when compensation increases.
coach_cues: Smooth first, bigger second.
Breathe into the range; do not force it.
Own the position before you move out of it.
Keep the motion where the drill is supposed to happen.
athlete_cues: Slow is smooth; smooth becomes athletic.
No pinching, no rushing, no sloppy reps.
Move like this range has to work in a game.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the transition if needed; exhale gently into the new range.
Keep the jaw and neck relaxed.
Do not hold the breath to force range.
common_faults: Chasing a bigger range by twisting through the low back or collapsing posture.
Rushing transitions until mobility turns into uncontrolled momentum.
Ignoring pinching, numbness, dizziness, or joint irritation.
Letting balance demands hide the actual mobility target.
quality_gate: No sharp pain or joint pinch.
Breathing stays calm and controlled.
The target joint action is visible without major compensation.
Final rep looks as controlled as the first rep.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or escalating discomfort.
Loss of posture for more than one rep in a row.
Athlete turns the drill into speed, fatigue, or competition instead of mobility.
Range decreases or compensation increases as reps continue.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use smaller ranges, fewer moving parts, and clear start/finish positions. Prioritize play-like smoothness without forcing depth or speed.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add a slightly longer path or step-through rhythm only when posture and breathing stay organized.
scaling.teen: Connect the drill to the sport or lift that follows. Keep the intent crisp and avoid turning warm-up mobility into fatigue volume.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use support and conservative range until the athlete can identify the intended joint action and avoid compensations.
scaling.adult_advanced: Add end-range pauses, active lift-offs, or flowing transitions while preserving low RPE and clean mechanics.
scaling.older_adult: Use extra support, smaller ranges, slower tempo, and longer setup time. Avoid floor transitions when getting up and down is the limiting factor.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use clinician guidance when relevant. Avoid breath holding, uncomfortable prone/supine positions, and pressure symptoms; choose supported standing or side-lying options when needed.

Set the ribs over the pelvis and keep the neck relaxed before initiating movement.
Use support, a wall, a mat, or a shorter range if balance or joint comfort limits quality.
Bias the drill toward shoulder extension, thoracic rotation, hip extension, and anterior-chain opening, not toward maximum speed or fatigue.
execution_steps: Move into the first position slowly enough to feel where range is available.
Breathe through the transition and keep the target joints moving without borrowing motion from the low back or neck.
Pause briefly at the usable end range, then return or step through with the same alignment.
Repeat only while the motion gets cleaner or stays clean; stop when compensation increases.
coach_cues: Smooth first, bigger second.
Breathe into the range; do not force it.
Own the position before you move out of it.
Keep the motion where the drill is supposed to happen.
athlete_cues: Slow is smooth; smooth becomes athletic.
No pinching, no rushing, no sloppy reps.
Move like this range has to work in a game.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the transition if needed; exhale gently into the new range.
Keep the jaw and neck relaxed.
Do not hold the breath to force range.
common_faults: Chasing a bigger range by twisting through the low back or collapsing posture.
Rushing transitions until mobility turns into uncontrolled momentum.
Ignoring pinching, numbness, dizziness, or joint irritation.
Letting balance demands hide the actual mobility target.
quality_gate: No sharp pain or joint pinch.
Breathing stays calm and controlled.
The target joint action is visible without major compensation.
Final rep looks as controlled as the first rep.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or escalating discomfort.
Loss of posture for more than one rep in a row.
Athlete turns the drill into speed, fatigue, or competition instead of mobility.
Range decreases or compensation increases as reps continue.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use smaller ranges, fewer moving parts, and clear start/finish positions. Prioritize play-like smoothness without forcing depth or speed.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add a slightly longer path or step-through rhythm only when posture and breathing stay organized.
scaling.teen: Connect the drill to the sport or lift that follows. Keep the intent crisp and avoid turning warm-up mobility into fatigue volume.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use support and conservative range until the athlete can identify the intended joint action and avoid compensations.
scaling.adult_advanced: Add end-range pauses, active lift-offs, or flowing transitions while preserving low RPE and clean mechanics.
scaling.older_adult: Use extra support, smaller ranges, slower tempo, and longer setup time. Avoid floor transitions when getting up and down is the limiting factor.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use clinician guidance when relevant. Avoid breath holding, uncomfortable prone/supine positions, and pressure symptoms; choose supported standing or side-lying options when needed.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by joint history, symptoms, sport demands, training age, confidence, and available range rather than gender.
doNotUseWhen: Sharp pain, joint pinching, numbness, tingling, or dizziness appears.
The athlete cannot control the starting position even after regression.
The session needs immediate high-output work and this drill would become a long, fatigue-creating block.
The athlete treats the drill as passive stretching or a race instead of active mobility.
mediaInternalNotes: Film one clean regression and one clean progression.
Emphasize position-access work, not passive stretching for its own sake.
Best before related strength, speed, skill, field, court, tumbling, or game prep.

Athlete can breathe calmly and control the starting position.

Symptoms that increase with the drill after range is reduced.
Loss of balance or support that makes the position unsafe.

cards.name: Bench T-Spine Extension Mobilization
cards.cardSummary: Dynamic mobility drill that opens thoracic extension, lat length, and overhead shoulder flexion. Use it when the athlete needs usable range of motion that carries into warm-ups, workouts, or games.
cards.bestPlacement: Prepare & Access phase after a brief temperature raise and before higher-intent skill, speed, strength, or sport work that needs thoracic extension, lat length, and overhead shoulder flexion.
cards.description: Kneel in front of a bench, place elbows or hands on the bench, sit the hips back, and breathe the chest toward the floor without low-back collapse.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Mobilize. Coach slow-to-smooth motion, clean breathing, and position ownership. This is flexibility combined with motion; stop before the athlete turns it into fatigue, passive stretching, or sloppy range chasing.
cards.athleteLanguage: Move smoothly, breathe through the range, and own the position you create.
cards.whyItWorks: The drill exposes thoracic extension, lat length, and overhead shoulder flexion while the athlete is actively controlling posture, breathing, and transitions. cards.commonMisuse: Rushing for reps, forcing end range through pinching, or turning the drill into a conditioning station. The goal is smoother access and control, not exhaustion or passive stretching for time.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by range, support, tempo, and total volume before adding complexity. Athletes should keep breathing, alignment, and repeatable motion as the progression standard.

cards.commonMisuse: Rushing for reps, forcing end range through pinching, or turning the drill into a conditioning station. The goal is smoother access and control, not exhaustion or passive stretching for time.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by range, support, tempo, and total volume before adding complexity. Athletes should keep breathing, alignment, and repeatable motion as the progression standard.

movementRequirements.breathing_demand: controlled nasal or relaxed diaphragmatic breathing

coachingExecution.movement_description: Move from the floor through a shin box or lunge transition to standing, then reverse the path with control and smooth breathing.
setup: Start in a pain-free range with enough space to move slowly and reset between reps.
Set the ribs over the pelvis and keep the neck relaxed before initiating movement.
Use support, a wall, a mat, or a shorter range if balance or joint comfort limits quality.
Bias the drill toward hip rotation, trunk control, shoulder support, and athletic transition ability, not toward maximum speed or fatigue.
execution_steps: Move into the first position slowly enough to feel where range is available.
Breathe through the transition and keep the target joints moving without borrowing motion from the low back or neck.
Pause briefly at the usable end range, then return or step through with the same alignment.
Repeat only while the motion gets cleaner or stays clean; stop when compensation increases.
coach_cues: Smooth first, bigger second.
Breathe into the range; do not force it.
Own the position before you move out of it.
Keep the motion where the drill is supposed to happen.
athlete_cues: Slow is smooth; smooth becomes athletic.
No pinching, no rushing, no sloppy reps.
Move like this range has to work in a game.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the transition if needed; exhale gently into the new range.
Keep the jaw and neck relaxed.
Do not hold the breath to force range.
common_faults: Chasing a bigger range by twisting through the low back or collapsing posture.
Rushing transitions until mobility turns into uncontrolled momentum.
Ignoring pinching, numbness, dizziness, or joint irritation.
Letting balance demands hide the actual mobility target.
quality_gate: No sharp pain or joint pinch.
Breathing stays calm and controlled.
The target joint action is visible without major compensation.
Final rep looks as controlled as the first rep.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or escalating discomfort.
Loss of posture for more than one rep in a row.
Athlete turns the drill into speed, fatigue, or competition instead of mobility.
Range decreases or compensation increases as reps continue.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use smaller ranges, fewer moving parts, and clear start/finish positions. Prioritize play-like smoothness without forcing depth or speed.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add a slightly longer path or step-through rhythm only when posture and breathing stay organized.
scaling.teen: Connect the drill to the sport or lift that follows. Keep the intent crisp and avoid turning warm-up mobility into fatigue volume.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use support and conservative range until the athlete can identify the intended joint action and avoid compensations.
scaling.adult_advanced: Add end-range pauses, active lift-offs, or flowing transitions while preserving low RPE and clean mechanics.
scaling.older_adult: Use extra support, smaller ranges, slower tempo, and longer setup time. Avoid floor transitions when getting up and down is the limiting factor.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use clinician guidance when relevant. Avoid breath holding, uncomfortable prone/supine positions, and pressure symptoms; choose supported standing or side-lying options when needed.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by joint history, symptoms, sport demands, training age, confidence, and available range rather than gender.
doNotUseWhen: Sharp pain, joint pinching, numbness, tingling, or dizziness appears.
The athlete cannot control the starting position even after regression.
The session needs immediate high-output work and this drill would become a long, fatigue-creating block.
The athlete treats the drill as passive stretching or a race instead of active mobility.
mediaInternalNotes: Film one clean regression and one clean progression.
Emphasize position-access work, not passive stretching for its own sake.
Best before related strength, speed, skill, field, court, tumbling, or game prep.

readiness_checks: No sharp pain, numbness, tingling, or dizziness before starting.
Athlete can breathe calmly and control the starting position.
Athlete has enough space and support to perform the drill without rushing.
contraindications: Acute joint injury in the target region without clearance.
Symptoms that increase with the drill after range is reduced.
Loss of balance or support that makes the position unsafe.
